sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: ἀνθʼ ὧν , βιασθεὶς πολλὰ κἀντιβάς , μόλις
sections.translation:
For that reason , under fierce constraint and with much resistance , at last he sacrificed her — but it was not for the sake of Menelaus .
But suppose — for I will make your own plea — suppose that the motive of his deed was to benefit his brother . Should you have killed him because of that ? Under what sort of law ?

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: ὅρα τιθεῖσα τόνδε τὸν νόμον βροτοῖς
sections.translation:
See that by laying down such a law for men , you do not lay down trouble and remorse for yourself . For , if we are to take blood for blood , you surely would be the first to die , if you were to meet with justice .
But consider whether this pretext is any excuse at all .

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: εἰ γὰρ θέλεις , δίδαξον ἀνθʼ ὅτου τανῦν
sections.translation: For tell me , if you please , what crime it is that you requite by doing the most shameless deeds of all : sharing your bed with that blood-guilty one , with whom you first destroyed my father and now bear his children

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: κἀξ εὐσεβῶν βλαστόντας ἐκβαλοῦσʼ ἔχεις .
sections.translation: while you have cast out the earlier born , the pious offspring of a pious marriage ? How can I commend these deeds ? Or will you claim that this , too , is recompense for your daughter ? No , it is a shameful plea , if you so plead , for there is nothing noble in marrying an enemy for a daughter ’ s sake .

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ γὰρ οὐδὲ νουθετεῖν ἔξεστί σε ,
sections.translation: But no , I can hardly even admonish you , when your every cry is that I slander my mother . I think , rather , that you are no less a mistress to me than a mother ; so lowly is the life that I live ,

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: πολλοῖς ἀεὶ ξυνοῦσα τοῦ τε συννόμου ·
sections.translation: ever beset with miseries come from you and your consort . And your other child , the exile who scarcely escaped your hand , poor Orestes , wastes away his unhappy life . You have often accused me of rearing him to punish your crime ,

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: ἔδρων ἄν , εὖ τοῦτʼ ἴσθι · τοῦδέ γʼ οὕνεκα
sections.translation: and I would have done so , if I could , you may be sure . As far as he is concerned , you can denounce me to all as disloyal , if you like , or loud-mouthed , or impudent . For if my nature is familiar with such wrongdoings , I hardly bring disgrace upon your nature .

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: ὁρῶ μένος πνέουσαν · εἰ δὲ σὺν δίκῃ
sections.translation: I see her breathing fury ; but whether justice is with her , her concern for this I see no longer .

sections.label: Section 612
sections.original: ποίας δʼ ἐμοὶ δεῖ πρός γε τήνδε φροντίδος ,
sections.translation: And what manner of concern should I use against her , who has abused her mother like this at her mature age ? Do you not think

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: χωρεῖν ἂν εἰς πᾶν ἔργον αἰσχύνης ἄτερ ;
sections.translation: that she would go forward to any deed without shame ?

sections.label: Section 616
sections.original: εὖ νυν ἐπίστω τῶνδέ μʼ αἰσχύνην ἔχειν ,
sections.translation: Now be assured that I do feel shame for it , though I seem not to you . I know that my behavior is unsuited to my age and inappropriate . But then the enmity I get from you and your

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: ἔργʼ ἐξαναγκάζει με ταῦτα δρᾶν βίᾳ ·
sections.translation: behavior compel me with harsh necessity to do this ; for reprehensible deeds are learned from reprehensible examples .

sections.label: Section 622
sections.original: ὦ θρέμμʼ ἀναιδές , ἦ σʼ ἐγὼ καὶ τἄμʼ ἔπη
sections.translation: You shameless creature ! Truly I and my speech and my deeds give you too much to talk about .

sections.label: Section 624
sections.original: σύ τοι λέγεις νιν , οὐκ ἐγώ · σὺ γὰρ ποεῖς
sections.translation: The words are yours , not mine ; for yours

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: τοὔργον · τὰ δʼ ἔργα τοὺς λόγους εὑρίσκεται .
sections.translation: are the deeds , and they find their own expression .

sections.label: Section 626
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ μὰ τὴν δέσποιναν Ἄρτεμιν θράσους
sections.translation: Now by our mistress Artemis , you shall not escape the consequences of this audacity once Aegisthus returns .

sections.label: Section 628
sections.original: ὁρᾷς ; πρὸς ὀργὴν ἐκφέρει , μεθεῖσά μοι
sections.translation: You see ? You are driven to rage and , even though you grant me free speech , you have no patience to listen .

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: οὔκουν ἐάσεις οὐδʼ ὑπʼ εὐφήμου βοῆς
sections.translation: Will you not allow me to sacrifice without ominous shouting , when I have permitted you to say anything and everything you wished ?

sections.label: Section 632
sections.original: ἐῶ , κελεύω , θῦε · μηδʼ ἐπαιτιῶ
sections.translation: I allow it ; I exhort you to it : sacrifice ! But do not blame my voice , for I would not say another word .

sections.label: Section 634
sections.original: ἔπαιρε δὴ σὺ θύμαθʼ ἡ παροῦσά μοι
sections.translation: Raise then , my attendant , the offerings

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: πάγκαρπʼ , ἄνακτι τῷδʼ ὅπως λυτηρίους
sections.translation: of many fruits , so that I may uplift my prayers for release from my present fears to this image of our King . Please , O Phoebus our defender , may you now listen to my prayer , though it is muffled ; for I do not make my plea among friends , nor does it suit me to unfold it all

sections.label: Section 640
sections.original: πρὸς φῶς παρούσης τῆσδε πλησίας ἐμοί ,
sections.translation: to the light while she stands near me , lest by her malice and a cry of her clamorous tongue she sow reckless rumors through the whole city . Nevertheless , hear me thus , since in this way I will speak . That vision which I saw last night

sections.label: Section 645
sections.original: δισσῶν ὀνείρων , ταῦτά μοι , Λύκειʼ ἄναξ ,
sections.translation: in ambiguous dreams — if its appearance was to my good , grant , Lycean king , that it be fulfilled ; but if to my harm , then hurl it back upon those who would harm me . And if any are plotting to eject me by treachery from my present prosperity , do not permit them .

sections.label: Section 650
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὧδέ μʼ αἰεὶ ζῶσαν ἀβλαβεῖ βίῳ
sections.translation: Rather grant that living forever unharmed as I am I may govern the house of the sons of Atreus and their throne , sharing prosperous days with the friends who share them now , and with those of my children who feel no enmity or bitterness towards me .

sections.label: Section 655
sections.original: ταῦτʼ , ὦ Λύκειʼ Ἄπολλον , ἵλεως κλύων
sections.translation: O Lycean Apollo , hear these prayers with favor , and grant them to us all just as we ask ! As for all my other prayers , though I am silent , I judge that you , a god , must know them , since it is appropriate that Zeus ’ s children see all .

sections.label: Section 660
sections.original: ξέναι γυναῖκες , πῶς ἂν εἰδείην σαφῶς
sections.translation: Foreign ladies , how might I know for certain if this be the palace of the king Aegisthus ?

sections.label: Section 662
sections.original: τάδʼ ἐστίν , ὦ ξένʼ · αὐτὸς ᾔκασας καλῶς .
sections.translation: This is the palace , stranger ; you yourself have guessed correctly .

sections.label: Section 663
sections.original: ἦ καὶ δάμαρτα τήνδʼ ἐπεικάζων κυρῶ
sections.translation: And am I on target when I surmise that this lady is his wife ? She is indeed regal to look upon .

sections.label: Section 665
sections.original: μάλιστα πάντων · ἥδε σοι κείνη πάρα .
sections.translation: Exactly right ; this is she before you .

sections.label: Section 666
sections.original: ὦ χαῖρʼ , ἄνασσα · σοὶ φέρων ἥκω λόγους
sections.translation: Greetings , royal lady ! I bring happy news to you and to Aegisthus from a friend .

sections.label: Section 668
sections.original: ἐδεξάμην τὸ ῥηθέν · εἰδέναι δέ σου
sections.translation: I welcome your greeting ; but I would like first to know who may have sent you .

sections.label: Section 670
sections.original: Φανοτεὺς ὁ Φωκεύς , πρᾶγμα πορσύνων μέγα .
sections.translation: Phanoteus the Phocian provided me the weighty task .

sections.label: Section 671
sections.original: τὸ ποῖον , ὦ ξένʼ ; εἰπέ · παρὰ φίλου γὰρ ὢν
sections.translation: And what is it , sir ? Tell me . Coming from a friend you will bring , I know , a kindly message .

sections.label: Section 673
sections.original: τέθνηκʼ Ὀρέστης · ἐν βραχεῖ ξυνθεὶς λέγω .
sections.translation: Orestes is dead , to put it briefly .

sections.label: Section 674
sections.original: οἲ ʼγὼ τάλαινʼ , ὄλωλα τῇδʼ ἐν ἡμέρᾳ .
sections.translation: Oh , miserable me ! My ruin comes today !

sections.label: Section 675
sections.original: τί φής , τί φής , ὦ ξεῖνε ; μὴ ταύτης κλύε .
sections.translation: What , friend ? What did you say ? Do not listen to her !

sections.label: Section 676
sections.original: θανόντʼ Ὀρέστην νῦν τε καὶ πάλαι λέγω .
sections.translation: I said , and say again , Orestes is dead .

sections.label: Section 677
sections.original: ἀπωλόμην δύστηνος , οὐδέν εἰμʼ ἔτι .
sections.translation: This is my wretched end ! I am no more !

sections.label: Section 678
sections.original: σὺ μὲν τὰ σαυτῆς πρᾶσσʼ , ἐμοὶ δὲ σύ , ξένε ,
sections.translation: You , go about your business ! But you , sir , tell me exactly in what manner he was destroyed .

sections.label: Section 680
sections.original: κἀπεμπόμην πρὸς ταῦτα καὶ τὸ πᾶν φράσω .
sections.translation: I was sent for that purpose , and will tell you all . Having gone to the shrine which is Greece ’ s common glory in order to compete for Delphi ’ s prizes and having heard the herald ’ s loud summons to the foot-race , the first contest ,

sections.label: Section 685
sections.original: εἰσῆλθε λαμπρός , πᾶσι τοῖς ἐκεῖ σέβας ·
sections.translation: he entered the lists , a brilliant form , a wonder in the eyes of all there . When he had finished the race at the point where it began , he went out with the glorious honor of victory . To say the most with the least words , I do not know the man whose deeds and triumphs have matched his .

sections.label: Section 690
sections.original: ἓν δʼ ἴσθʼ · ὅσων γὰρ εἰσεκήρυξαν βραβῆς
sections.translation: But this one thing you must know : in all the contests that the judges announced , he carried away the prize , and men deemed him happy as often as the herald proclaimed him an Argive , by name Orestes , son of

sections.label: Section 695
sections.original: Ἀγαμέμνονος στράτευμʼ ἀγείραντός ποτε .
sections.translation:
Agamemnon , who once marshalled Greece ’ s famous expedition .
So far Orestes fared as I described . But when a god sends harm , not even the strong man can escape . For on another day , when with the rising sun there was held the race of the swift-footed horses ,

sections.label: Section 700
sections.original: εἰσῆλθε πολλῶν ἁρματηλατῶν μέτα .
sections.translation: he entered it along with many charioteers . One was an Achaean , one from Sparta ; two masters of yoked cars were Libyans ; Orestes , driving Thessalian mares , came fifth among them ; the sixth was from Aetolia ,

sections.label: Section 705
sections.original: ξανθαῖσι πώλοις · ἕβδομος Μάγνης ἀνήρ ·
sections.translation: with chestnut colts ; a Magnesian was the seventh ; the eighth , with white horses , was of Aenian stock ; the ninth hailed from Athens , built of gods ; there was a Boeotian too , making the tenth chariot . They took their stations where the appointed umpires

sections.label: Section 710
sections.original: κλήροις ἔπηλαν καὶ κατέστησαν δίφρους ,
sections.translation: placed them by lot and ranged the cars . Then at the sound of the bronze trumpet , they started . All shouted to their horses , and shook the reins in their hands ; the whole course was filled with the clatter of rattling chariots ; and the dust flew upward .

sections.label: Section 715
sections.original: φορεῖθʼ · ὁμοῦ δὲ πάντες ἀναμεμιγμένοι
sections.translation: All of them in a confused throng kept plying their goads unsparingly , so that one of them might pass the wheel-hubs and the snorting steeds of his rivals ; for both at their backs and at their rolling wheels the breath of the horses foamed and smattered .

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: κεῖνος δʼ ὑπʼ αὐτὴν ἐσχάτην στήλην ἔχων
sections.translation: Orestes , driving close to the near edge of the turning-post , almost grazed it with his wheel each time and , giving rein to the trace-horse on the right , he checked the horse on the inner side . To this point , all the chariots still stood upright . But then the Aenian ’ s

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: πῶλοι βίᾳ φέρουσιν · ἐκ δʼ ὑποστροφῆς
sections.translation: hard-mouthed colts carried him out of control as they passed out of the turn from the sixth into the seventh lap and dashed their foreheads against the rig of the Barcaean . Next , as a result of this one mishap , the cars kept smashing and colliding with each other , and the whole

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: ναυαγίων Κρισαῖον ἱππικῶν πέδον .
sections.translation: race-ground of Crisa swelled with shipwrecked chariots .

sections.label: Section 731
sections.original: γνοὺς δʼ οὑξ Ἀθηνῶν δεινὸς ἡνιοστρόφος
sections.translation: Seeing this , the clever charioteer from Athens drew aside and paused , allowing the equestrian flood to pass in mid-crest . Orestes was driving last , keeping his horses

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: πώλους Ὀρέστης , τῷ τέλει πίστιν φέρων ·
sections.translation: behind , as his trust was in the race ’ s end . But when he sees that the Athenian is alone left in , he sends a shrill cry ringing through the ears of his swift colts , and gives chase . Bringing yoke level with yoke the two of them raced , first one man , then the other ,

sections.label: Section 740
sections.original: κάρα προβάλλων ἱππικῶν ὀχημάτων .
sections.translation: showing his head in front of the other ’ s chariot . Up to now the ill-fated Orestes had driven upright safely through every circuit , upright in his upright car . But then he slackened his left rein while the horse was turning and unwittingly struck the edge of the pillar ,

sections.label: Section 745
sections.original: παίσας · ἔθραυσε δʼ ἄξονος μέσας χνόας
sections.translation:
breaking the axle-box in two . He spilled forward over the chariot-rail and was caught in the trim reins , and as he fell to the ground , his colts were scattered into the middle of the course .
But when the crowd saw that he had fallen

sections.label: Section 750
sections.original: δίφρων , ἀνωλόλυξε τὸν νεανίαν ,
sections.translation: from the chariot , a cry of pity went up for the young man who had done such deeds and was allotted such bad fortune — now dashed against the earth , now tossed with his feet to the sky until the charioteers with difficulty reigned in the gallop of his horses and

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: ἔλυσαν αἱματηρόν , ὥστε μηδένα
sections.translation: freed him , so covered with blood that no friend who saw it would have known the pitiful corpse . Immediately they burned him on a pyre , and chosen men of Phocis now bring the sad dust of that mighty form in a small urn of bronze ,

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: ὅπως πατρῴας τύμβον ἐκλάχῃ χθονός .
sections.translation:
so that he may find due burial in his fatherland .
Such is my story — it is grievous even to hear , but for us witnesses who looked on , it was the greatest of sorrows that these eyes have seen .

sections.label: Section 764
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ · τὸ πᾶν δὴ δεσπόταισι τοῖς πάλαι
sections.translation: Oh , sorrow ! It seems now that all the stock of our ancient masters

sections.label: Section 765
sections.original: πρόρριζον , ὡς ἔοικεν , ἔφθαρται γένος .
sections.translation: has been leveled clean down to the roots .

sections.label: Section 766
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ , τί ταῦτα , πότερον εὐτυχῆ λέγω ,
sections.translation: O Zeus , how shall I name this news — fortunate ? Or terrible , but beneficial ? It is a bitter thing , when by my own misery I preserve my life .
